creator: cem@freebsd.org
text:
Notably, close(2) manual page does not document that all cached data associated with the file must be synced by the time it returns, nor does it document an EIO error return.  The main function of close() is to deallocate the filehandle associated with a given fd number.  On FreeBSD, IIRC it always succeeds at that, even if EINTR is returned.

POSIX 2008-2017 (latest, I think) has a longer description of the behavior POSIX systems are allowed or required to implement for close(2): http://pubs.opengroup.org/onlinepubs/9699919799/functions/close.html

It notes that EIO /may/ be returned, but if it is, the state of fildes is unspecified.

It notes that "The close() operation itself need not block awaiting such [pending asynchronous] I/O completion."

There are some special semantics required for sockets, STREAMS, ttys, pipes and FIFOs, but happily we only implement regular files and directories.

I suspect this may be "Not a Bug."
